petitioner_background: The petitioner is an executive sous chef seeking EB-1A classification as a noncitizen with extraordinary ability. He has been in O-1 nonimmigrant visa status since June 2018, working first at a hotel in Puerto Rico and then at another hotel location. Prior to entering the United States, he worked as an executive sous chef at a establishment in South America, and he also has prior work experience in Guatemala. He intends to continue his work in the same culinary field in the United States.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner provided his 2022 income tax return and Form W-2 showing an income of $71,085 for his position as executive sous chef. He also submitted the May 2021 Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics (OEWS) page for chefs and head cooks showing the annual mean wage for the occupation to be $56,920. He also asserted that his income of $82,000 at his current hotel was more than $25,000 higher than the 2021 annual mean wage for chefs nationwide.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner did not sufficiently document how his salary compares to other hotel executive sous chefs. The 2021 OEWS information provided only the annual mean wage, which is a benchmark for typical wages, but a petitioner must provide appropriate evidence such as geographical or position-appropriate compensation surveys. The mean salary information was for a nationwide survey of head cooks and chefs in all settings (both hotels and otherwise) and across all geographic areas (both major metropolitan areas and those that are not). The AAO also noted that the same OEWS survey showed head chefs and cooks in the top 90% nationwide earned $84,570, meaning the petitioner's 2021 salary of $71,085 was more than $10,000 less than those top earners. Regarding his $82,000 claim, the petitioner did not provide updated survey information to support the assertion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted numerous articles, social media posts, and still images from TV appearances. This included ten articles showcasing his recipes in the food section of the Guatemalan newspaper El Periodico (with his photograph and his employer's business logo), articles in National News Guatemala and Prensa Libre promoting the hotel restaurant where he worked, articles in Contrapoder and Semana's segment displaying his recipes or covering events he took part in (including a charity dinner), an article in the culture section of Metro Libre (2019) about his participation in a Guatemalan food festival along with two other chefs (containing a short quote from him about Mayan cooking techniques and ingredients), articles discussing restaurants where he worked, food trends (sushi, poke bowls, buffets, pizzerias), special events, and food offerings in the hotel/tourism industry. He also submitted still images from TV appearances on NTN24, Nuestra TeleNoticias, TV Azteca, and Telemetro.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the published material was not 'about' the petitioner within the meaning of the regulation. Published material should be about the person relating to the person's work in the field, not about the person's employer or another organization the person is associated with. Many articles promoted the hotel restaurant where he worked rather than being about him. The articles discussed restaurants, food trends, special events, and food offerings in the industry, but were not about him. The Metro Libre article highlighted his participation along with two other chefs but was about the food festival event. The TV appearances' still images showed the coverage was about his employers' restaurants or promotional events at the hotel's restaurants, not about him or his work. The petitioner's assertion that 'published recipes included in the various newspaper and magazine articles are major achievements' lacked context, and even if true, he still had to meet the plain language of the regulation. The AAO cited Noroozi v. Napolitano and Negro-Plumpe v. Okin, noting that articles only briefly mentioning a person or focusing on an employer/show are not 'about' the person.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted letters of reference and organizational charts showing that at the Puerto Rico hotel, his position was third following the 'General Manager' and 'Executive Chef and Food and Beverage Manager,' and that he supervised 'Sous Chef,' 'Jr. Sous Chef,' 'Banquets Chef,' 'Restaurant Chef Supervisor,' 'Banquets Chef Supervisor,' and 'Cooks Level I-III.' The organizational chart for the other hotel showed him in a similar relative position within the hotel's food and beverage department (FBD). To show distinguished reputation, he provided 2018, 2019, 2020, and 2021 'Recognition' lists of awards given to the hotel chain, printouts showing amenities (restaurants, pools, rooms), a 2020 Annual Report, SEC annual report for 2020, information about the hotel's amenities, an article highlighting the hotel's history in downtown Texas, and a letter from an individual who hired and worked with him stating the FBD is 'expected to increase by $2M by the end of 2023, due to the growth of tourism in the city.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner's evidence, including reference letters, established that he performed in a critical role within the restaurants and FBDs of the hotels where he worked. However, he did not show that any of the FBDs within these hotel chains have a 'distinguished reputation.' The recognition lists did not recognize or concern the hotel's restaurants or FBDs but instead recognized the hotel chain as a whole. The amenity information, annual reports, and history article did not establish the distinguished reputation of any of the hotels' FBDs. The letter about projected FBD revenue growth did not address the FBD's distinguished reputation and instead linked growth to tourism and the popularity of the Texas city. The AAO emphasized that since the petitioner claimed a leading/critical role for the restaurant or FBD within the hotel, he must establish the distinguished reputation of the FBD and not the hotel itself.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal because the petitioner failed to establish that he met at least three of the ten evidentiary criteria required under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3). Since he did not meet the minimum three criteria, the AAO did not need to provide a final merits determination as referenced in Kazarian. The AAO reserved ruling on two additional claimed criteria (lesser awards and commercial success) because even if met, he still could not numerically satisfy the three-criteria requirement. The AAO also considered but rejected the petitioner's comparable evidence argument regarding reference letters, finding he failed to explain why the regulatory criteria did not readily apply to his occupation. The AAO acknowledged his O-1 nonimmigrant status since 2018 as relevant but found the evidentiary deficiencies did not support EB-1 classification, emphasizing that extraordinary ability is an extremely restrictive designation reserved for those at the very top of their field.

Rijal v. USCIS, 772 F. Supp. 2d 1339 (W.D. Wash. 2011)

Grimson v. INS, 934 F. Supp. 965, 968 (N.D. Ill. 1996)

Matter of Obaigbena, 19 I&N Dec. 533, 534 n.2 (BIA 1988)
Hamal v. Dep't of Homeland Sec. (Hamal II), No. 19-cv-2534, 2021 WL 2338316 (D.D.C. June 8, 2021)
Hamal v. Dep't of Homeland Sec. (Hamal I), No. 19-cv-2534, 2020 WL 2934954 (D.D.C. June 3, 2020)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The petitioner waived the exhibition criterion (8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(vii)) by not raising it on appeal after bringing it up in the RFE response. The petitioner incorrectly argued that because the Director only evaluated three of the five asserted criteria, USCIS must have considered that he established the other two criteria by a preponderance of the evidence; the AAO rejected this argument. The Director omitted evaluation of two claimed criteria (lesser awards and commercial success) but the AAO declined to remand because the petitioner could not meet the minimum three criteria regardless. The AAO reserved ruling on these two additional criteria per INS v. Bagamasbad.
